era_context: 1640 was a pivotal year in European and global history, marked by the Portuguese Restoration, the beginning of the crisis that would lead to the English Civil War, the continuation of the Thirty Years' War, and the final years of the Ming Dynasty in China. This was an era of dynastic instability, religious conflict, and the emergence of new political systems in Europe and Asia.

events.title: Portuguese Restoration (Restauração Portuguesa)
events.description: The Portuguese Restoration, also known as the Portuguese Acclamation, occurred on December 1, 1640, when Portuguese nobles and the Duke of Braganza led a successful revolt against Spanish Habsburg rule. Portugal had been under Spanish rule since 1580, following the Portuguese succession crisis. The restoration of Portuguese independence was a major political event that demonstrated the fragility of Spanish power and the strength of Portuguese national identity. The Duke of Braganza became King John IV of Portugal, establishing the House of Braganza on the Portuguese throne. The Portuguese Restoration War (1640–1668) would follow, with Portuguese forces fighting to maintain their independence from Spain. The restoration was a significant blow to Spanish power and contributed to the decline of Spanish hegemony in Europe.
key_figures: John IV of Portugal (Duke of Braganza)
Cardinal Richelieu (French chief minister, who supported Portuguese independence)

sources.name: Portuguese and Spanish court records and diplomatic correspondence
Contemporary chronicles and later Portuguese and Spanish histories

events.certainty_note: The Portuguese Restoration on December 1, 1640, is one of the most well-documented events of the period, with the date firmly established in both Portuguese and Spanish records.
cross_references: Leads to the Portuguese Restoration War (1640–1668)
Contributes to the decline of Spanish hegemony in Europe

events.title: Long Parliament convened in England
events.description: The Long Parliament was convened by King Charles I on November 3, 1640, in response to the crisis caused by the Bishops' Wars and the need to raise funds for military campaigns. The Long Parliament would prove to be one of the most important parliaments in English history, as it would challenge royal authority, impeach royal ministers, and eventually lead to the English Civil War. The parliament was dominated by members who opposed royal policies and sought to limit the power of the Crown. The Long Parliament would remain in session (with interruptions) until 1660, making it one of the longest parliaments in English history. The convening of the Long Parliament marked the beginning of the political crisis that would lead to the English Civil War in 1642.
key_figures: Thomas Wentworth, 1st Earl of Strafford (royal minister, later executed)
events.certainty_note: The convening of the Long Parliament on November 3, 1640, is one of the most well-documented events of the period, with the date firmly established in parliamentary records.
cross_references: Leads to the English Civil War (1642–1651)
Leads to the execution of Charles I (1649)

events.title: Continued warfare in the Thirty Years' War
events.region: Europe (Holy Roman Empire, France, Sweden, Spain)
events.description: The Thirty Years' War continued in 1640 with ongoing campaigns in Germany, the Low Countries, and other parts of Europe. French forces, under the command of the young Duke of Enghien (later the Great Condé), were engaged in campaigns against Spanish and Imperial forces. Swedish forces were also active in northern Germany. The war was in its final phase, with the major powers beginning to consider peace negotiations. The year 1640 saw continued military operations that would eventually lead to the Treaty of Westphalia in 1648.
key_figures: Louis II, Prince of Condé (Duke of Enghien)
events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Thirty Years' War in 1640 is well-documented in contemporary military and diplomatic records.
cross_references: Part of the Thirty Years' War (1618–1648)
Leads to the Treaty of Westphalia (1648)

events.title: Ming Dynasty in final decline; Chongzhen Emperor's authority weakening
events.description: In 1640, the Ming Dynasty was in its final years, with the Chongzhen Emperor facing severe challenges from internal rebellion and external threats. Li Zicheng's rebel forces were gaining strength in central and northern China, and the Manchu threat from the north was increasing. The Ming government was financially exhausted and militarily weakened. By 1644, Li Zicheng would capture Beijing, leading to the Chongzhen Emperor's suicide and the collapse of Ming rule. The year 1640 represented a critical moment in the transition from Ming to Qing rule, with the old dynasty in its death throes and new powers rising to challenge its authority.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general situation of Ming decline in 1640 is well-documented, though specific events and dates for this year are less precisely recorded than for 1644.
cross_references: Leads to the fall of Beijing and the Ming Dynasty (1644)
Part of the broader Qing conquest of China (1644–1683)

events.title: Bishops' Wars in Scotland
events.description: The Bishops' Wars (1639–1640) were a series of conflicts between the Scottish Covenanters and King Charles I over religious governance in Scotland. The wars were fought over the issue of whether the Church of Scotland should have bishops (as Charles I desired) or be governed by a Presbyterian system (as the Covenanters preferred). The First Bishops' War (1639) ended with the Treaty of Berwick, and the Second Bishops' War (1640) resulted in a Scottish victory. The Scottish forces defeated the English army at the Battle of Newburn in August 1640, demonstrating Scottish military strength and English weakness. The Bishops' Wars contributed to the political crisis in England and the convening of the Long Parliament in November 1640.
key_figures: King Charles I (English and Scottish monarch)
Archibald Campbell, 1st Marquess of Argyll (Scottish leader)

sources.name: Scottish and English military records and correspondence

events.certainty_note: The Bishops' Wars are well-documented in both Scottish and English sources. The Battle of Newburn in August 1640 is firmly dated.
cross_references: Leads to the convening of the Long Parliament (November 1640)
Contributes to the English Civil War (1642–1651)

events.title: Founding of the Académie française's dictionary project
events.description: The Académie française, founded in 1635 by Cardinal Richelieu, undertook the project of creating a comprehensive French dictionary in 1640. The dictionary project was intended to standardize and codify the French language, reflecting the Académie's mission to promote French culture and language. The project would take many decades to complete, with the first edition of the Dictionnaire de l'Académie française published in 1694. The dictionary project represented an important moment in the development of French language and culture during the reign of Louis XIII and the early reign of Louis XIV.
key_figures: Cardinal Richelieu (founder of the Académie française)

sources.name: Later historical accounts of the Académie française

events.certainty_note: The exact date of the beginning of the dictionary project is sometimes given as 1639 or 1640 in different sources. The project was ongoing throughout the 1640s and beyond.
cross_references: Part of the development of French culture and language in the 17th century
Leads to the publication of the Dictionnaire de l'Académie française (1694)

disconfirming_evidence: The Portuguese Restoration is sometimes portrayed as a simple nationalist uprising, but modern scholarship emphasizes the role of French support and the complex political motivations of the Portuguese nobility. The Long Parliament is sometimes seen as a unified body opposing the Crown, but modern historians recognize significant divisions within Parliament over the extent of reform and the proper relationship between Crown and Parliament. The Bishops' Wars are sometimes portrayed as purely religious conflicts, but modern scholarship emphasizes the political and constitutional dimensions of the disputes. The significance of the Bishops' Wars in leading to the English Civil War is sometimes overstated; while they contributed to the political crisis, other factors were also important.
historiographic_note: The year 1640 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly for England, Scotland, Portugal, and the Thirty Years' War. The main sources are contemporary political, military, and diplomatic records, correspondence, and memoirs. The bias is toward elite political and military history; social and economic history is less thoroughly documented. The English and Scottish sources are particularly rich for the Bishops' Wars and the convening of the Long Parliament. Portuguese sources document the Restoration, though Spanish sources provide a different perspective. Non-European regions are underrepresented in surviving sources, with the exception of Chinese records related to the Ming Dynasty's decline. The political crises in England and Scotland are particularly well-documented, while events in Asia, Africa, and the Americas are less thoroughly recorded in European archives.

graph_edges.note: The Portuguese Restoration in 1640 led to the Portuguese Restoration War (1640–1668).
The convening of the Long Parliament in 1640 led to the English Civil War in 1642.
The Bishops' Wars in 1640 led to the convening of the Long Parliament in November 1640.
The Ming Dynasty's decline in 1640 led to its collapse in 1644.
